# Project Charta
## Context and Scope

This project investigates patterns in cybersecurity incidents recorded between 2015 and 2024
in order to support clearer understanding and communication of cyber threat trends across
time, countries, industries, and attack characteristics.

The project is based on the Kaggle dataset *Global Cybersecurity Threats (2015–2024)*,
which contains 3,000 recorded incidents from 10 countries (Australia, Brazil, China,
France, Germany, India, Japan, Russia, UK, USA) and seven target industries (Banking,
Education, Government, Healthcare, IT, Retail, Telecommunications).

For each incident, the dataset includes contextual and analytical variables such as country,
industry, year, attack type, attack source, exploited vulnerability, defense mechanism,
financial loss in million USD, number of affected users, and incident resolution time in
hours.

The project focuses on identifying and communicating patterns in the recorded incidents
rather than making causal claims about the overall global cybersecurity situation. The scope
is therefore limited to comparative and descriptive analysis based on the variables available
in the dataset.

The project addresses the following questions:

- How have recorded cyber incidents evolved between 2015 and 2024 in terms of count,
  financial loss, affected users, and resolution time?
- Which countries and industries in the dataset experience the highest incident frequency,
  the greatest financial losses, and the largest numbers of affected users?
- Which attack types are most common, and which are associated with the highest losses,
  widest user impact, or longest resolution times?
- Which vulnerability types are most frequently exploited, and how do they relate to
  attack type, industry, and attack source?
- How are attack sources distributed across countries and industries, and do some countries
  or sectors show distinct threat profiles?
- How do defense mechanisms vary across incidents, and how do incident outcomes differ
  across defense mechanisms in terms of loss and resolution time?
- Which combinations of country, industry, attack type, and vulnerability appear most
  risk-intensive within the dataset?
- Are there sectors or countries with a mismatch between attack frequency and attack
  severity, for example many incidents but relatively lower losses, or fewer incidents
  with very high losses?

The expected benefit of the project is to transform a relatively complex incident dataset
into an accessible visual product that helps users quickly detect patterns, compare risk
dimensions, and communicate relevant findings to decision-makers.

The visualization product will be published as an interactive web-based report
on GitHub Pages as part of the project repository.

## Project Objectives and Success Criteria

The project aims to support cybersecurity analysts, IT security managers, and decision-makers
in exploring, understanding, and communicating important patterns in the incident dataset
more efficiently than would be possible from raw tabular data alone.

From the stakeholder perspective, the main objectives are:

- enable users to identify which countries, industries, attack types, and vulnerability
  categories are most strongly associated with incident frequency and incident severity
- enable users to compare incidents not only by count, but also by financial loss,
  number of affected users, and incident resolution time
- enable users to recognize how threat profiles differ across countries, industries,
  and time periods
- enable users to examine how attack type, attack source, vulnerability, and defense
  mechanism relate to one another within the recorded incidents
- enable analysts to extract evidence-based insights for reporting, benchmarking, and
  internal communication
- enable managers and other decision-makers to obtain a concise overview of high-risk
  patterns and their potential operational or financial relevance
- provide a trustworthy and interpretable visual basis for prioritization, discussion,
  and further investigation

Success criteria:

- A user with medium data literacy can identify the most affected countries or industries
  within 60 seconds of opening the visualization.
- A user can compare at least two dimensions of cyber risk, for example attack type and
  industry or defense mechanism and resolution time, without external assistance.
- The visualization makes it possible to distinguish between attack frequency and attack
  severity rather than reducing the analysis to incident counts alone.
- The main patterns across time, country, industry, attack type, vulnerability, and
  incident outcomes can be understood without prior knowledge of the raw dataset structure.
- The product supports both quick management-oriented orientation and more detailed
  analyst-oriented comparison.
- The visualization is readable and functional on standard desktop and laptop screens
  used in professional environments.

Out of scope:

- predictive modeling or forecasting of future cyber incidents
- real-time monitoring or live data integration
- causal claims about the effectiveness of specific defense mechanisms
- organization-level conclusions beyond the aggregated incident records in the dataset
- claims about the full global threat landscape beyond the countries and incidents
  represented in the data
- attribution claims beyond the attack source categories available in the dataset

## Stakeholder Analysis

The project involves several stakeholder groups with different goals, levels of influence,
and relationships to the final visualization product.

The primary stakeholders are **cybersecurity analysts and IT security managers**. They are
the main intended users of the visualization and use it to explore incident patterns,
compare threat profiles, identify high-risk combinations, and prepare reports or
recommendations. Their main interests are analytical depth, clarity, and efficient
comparison across dimensions such as year, country, industry, attack type, vulnerability,
defense mechanism, financial loss, affected users, and resolution time.

A secondary stakeholder group is **executive leadership**, such as a CISO, CTO, or other
management representatives. These stakeholders are less interested in detailed exploratory
analysis and more interested in a concise, trustworthy summary of the most important
patterns in the dataset. They use the visualization to support prioritization, internal
communication, strategic discussion, and resource allocation.

Another important stakeholder is the **project team** acting as data analysts and
visualization authors. The team has high influence over scope, analysis, design, and
implementation. Its role is to translate the available data into a coherent visual product
that serves both detailed analytical needs and high-level communication needs.

A further stakeholder group is **teaching staff / the project coach**, who are not the
main end users of the visualization but strongly influence the project through requirements,
feedback, and evaluation. Their interest lies in a well-structured, methodologically sound,
and clearly justified project outcome.

A peripheral stakeholder group consists of **external readers**, such as fellow students or
other viewers accessing the GitHub Pages site. They have little direct influence on the
project, but they still benefit from a clear, interpretable, and well-documented
presentation of the findings.

Overall, the stakeholder relationships are shaped by different levels of detail and
different decision contexts. Analysts and security managers require exploratory and
comparative views, executive stakeholders require condensed and decision-oriented summaries,
and teaching staff require transparency and methodological consistency. The project must
therefore function as a bridge between analysis, communication, and academic documentation.

```{=html}
<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; margin: 2rem 0;">
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 1200 560" width="100%" style="max-width: 1100px; height: auto; font-family: Arial, sans-serif;">

  <!-- Background circles -->
  <circle cx="620" cy="270" r="210" fill="#e8e8e8"></circle>
  <circle cx="620" cy="270" r="130" fill="#cfcfd1"></circle>
  <circle cx="620" cy="270" r="70" fill="#9fa0a4"></circle>

  <!-- Legend -->
  <circle cx="930" cy="85" r="12" fill="#e8e8e8"></circle>
  <text x="960" y="92" font-size="18">Peripheral stakeholders</text>

  <circle cx="930" cy="135" r="12" fill="#cfcfd1"></circle>
  <text x="960" y="142" font-size="18">Important stakeholders</text>

  <circle cx="930" cy="185" r="12" fill="#9fa0a4"></circle>
  <text x="960" y="192" font-size="18">Core stakeholders</text>

  <!-- Executive Leadership -->
  <text x="40" y="85" font-size="20">Executive Leadership</text>
  <line x1="255" y1="78" x2="620" y2="78" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"></line>
  <line x1="620" y1="78" x2="690" y2="120" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"></line>
  <circle cx="690" cy="120" r="6" fill="black"></circle>

  <!-- Teaching Staff / Coach -->
  <text x="40" y="145" font-size="20">Teaching Staff / Coach</text>
  <line x1="280" y1="138" x2="600" y2="138" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"></line>
  <line x1="600" y1="138" x2="655" y2="200" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"></line>
  <circle cx="655" cy="200" r="6" fill="black"></circle>

  <!-- Project Team -->
  <text x="40" y="205" font-size="20">Project Team</text>
  <line x1="170" y1="198" x2="560" y2="198" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"></line>
  <line x1="560" y1="198" x2="620" y2="235" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"></line>
  <circle cx="620" cy="235" r="6" fill="black"></circle>

  <!-- Cybersecurity Analysts / IT Security Managers -->
  <text x="40" y="285" font-size="20">Cybersecurity Analysts / IT Security Managers</text>
  <line x1="470" y1="278" x2="570" y2="278" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"></line>
  <line x1="570" y1="278" x2="620" y2="300" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"></line>
  <circle cx="620" cy="300" r="6" fill="black"></circle>

  <!-- External Readers -->
  <text x="40" y="365" font-size="20">External Readers</text>
  <line x1="205" y1="358" x2="500" y2="358" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"></line>
  <line x1="500" y1="358" x2="535" y2="335" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"></line>
  <circle cx="535" cy="335" r="6" fill="black"></circle>

</svg>
</div>
```

*Figure: Stakeholder map of the cybersecurity visualization project.*

## User Analysis

The visualization product is designed for two main user groups with different levels of
domain expertise, data literacy, and decision needs. The first persona represents a more
analytical and exploratory use case, while the second persona represents a more condensed
and decision-oriented use case. Together, they reflect the main stakeholder groups
identified in the project: operational security users and management-oriented users.

| Attribute | Persona 1: Sophia | Persona 2: Marcus |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Role | Senior Cybersecurity Analyst | IT Security Manager |
| Age | 34 | 48 |
| Domain expertise | High | Medium-high |
| Data literacy | High | Medium |
| Technical environment | Desktop workstation, occasionally laptop | Corporate laptop, occasionally tablet |
| Typical screen setup | 27" monitor, high-resolution display | 14" laptop screen |
| Frequency of use | Weekly | Monthly |
| Main use | Detailed exploration, comparison, reporting | Quick overview, prioritization, communication |
| Main need | Analytical depth and filtering flexibility | Clear summaries and rapid time-to-insight |

### Persona 1 — Sophia, Senior Cybersecurity Analyst

Sophia works in a security-focused environment and regularly prepares internal reporting,
trend analysis, and evidence-based recommendations. She is comfortable interpreting
multi-dimensional data and expects the visualization to support flexible comparison across
countries, industries, attack types, vulnerabilities, defense mechanisms, and incident
outcomes.

**User tasks (jobs)**

- **Functional:** Analyse attack trends by year, country, and industry; compare attack
  types and vulnerabilities; assess patterns in financial loss, affected users, and
  resolution time; examine how defense mechanisms relate to recorded outcomes.
- **Social:** Prepare reports and briefings for the CISO or IT leadership; communicate
  findings to colleagues and decision-makers; support internal prioritization discussions.
- **Emotional:** Feel confident that the analysis is evidence-based, credible, and not
  reliant on manual aggregation or intuition alone.

**Pains**

- Manually combining static tables and charts is time-consuming and increases the risk of
  inconsistent interpretation.
- Existing visual summaries often do not support cross-category comparison, for example
  across country, industry, and attack type at the same time.
- It is difficult to communicate more nuanced findings, such as differences between attack
  frequency and attack severity, to less technical audiences.
- Overly simplified dashboards may hide patterns she considers important.

**Gains**

- A single visual product that makes patterns across time, geography, sector, and threat
  characteristics easy to compare.
- Clear views of both **frequency** and **severity**, using not only incident count but
  also financial loss, affected users, and resolution time.
- Interactive filtering that helps her move quickly from a broad overview to a more focused
  comparison.
- Exportable or presentation-friendly outputs would further increase practical usefulness.

### Persona 2 — Marcus, IT Security Manager

Marcus is responsible for understanding relevant threat patterns at a higher level and for
communicating security priorities to management. He has strong practical knowledge of IT
security, but less time and less need for deep exploratory analysis than Sophia. He values
clarity, trustworthiness, and quick orientation.

**User tasks (jobs)**

- **Functional:** Get a quick overview of important threat developments; identify the most
  relevant industries, countries, attack types, and vulnerabilities; compare broad outcome
  measures such as financial loss or resolution time.
- **Social:** Communicate cyber risk patterns to senior management; justify security-related
  priorities or spending; align internal discussions around a shared evidence base.
- **Emotional:** Feel confident that his recommendations are supported by credible evidence
  and that he can explain them clearly without getting lost in technical detail.

**Pains**

- Complex dashboards with too many views or controls create friction and reduce trust.
- Time is limited, so he needs to understand the main message within a few minutes.
- Purely technical views do not always translate well into management communication.
- Without a clear overview, it is hard to distinguish what is frequent from what is truly severe.

**Gains**

- A clear entry point with concise summary views and a small number of well-chosen charts.
- Immediate visibility of the most affected sectors, attack types, or outcome patterns.
- Short explanatory text or annotations that help interpret the findings.
- A product that supports both fast orientation and follow-up discussion with colleagues
  or leadership.

### Design implications derived from the user analysis

The user analysis suggests that the visualization should combine **high-level clarity** with
**selective analytical depth**. It should therefore:

- provide an immediate overview for management-oriented users
- support comparison across multiple dimensions for analyst-oriented users
- distinguish clearly between **incident frequency** and **incident severity**
- remain readable on standard laptop screens while still working well on larger desktop displays
- use limited, meaningful interactivity rather than an overly complex dashboard structure

These implications directly inform the later visualization concept and support the goal of
building a product that is both analytically useful and easy to communicate.

## Situation Assessment

The project is based on a predefined public dataset and is developed within the framework
of a semester-long visualization project. The main challenge is not data access itself, but
the transformation of a multi-dimensional incident dataset into a clear, interpretable, and
well-structured visual product that supports both analytical exploration and communication.

### Resources

**Data**

The project uses the Kaggle dataset *Global Cybersecurity Threats (2015–2024)*. The dataset
contains 3,000 recorded incidents and provides a suitable basis for descriptive and
comparative analysis across multiple dimensions, including country, industry, attack type,
attack source, vulnerability, defense mechanism, financial loss, affected users, and
incident resolution time.

**Tools and infrastructure**

The project repository is managed on GitHub and documented as a Quarto project. The final
visualization product will be published via GitHub Pages. This setup supports version
control, reproducibility, collaborative editing, and transparent documentation of project
decisions.

**Team resources**

The project is carried out by a three-person team. This allows responsibilities to be split
across data preparation, analysis, visualization design, implementation, documentation, and
presentation, while still requiring close coordination across all project phases.

### Constraints

**Time constraints**

The project follows the semester schedule, with the draft project charta due in March 2026
and the final visualization product due toward the end of the semester. The available time
is sufficient for a well-scoped visual product, but not for an overly broad or technically
complex solution.

**Data constraints**

The dataset is suitable for comparative and descriptive analysis, but it does not support
causal claims, real-time monitoring, or conclusions about organizations not represented in
the incident records. The project must therefore stay within the limits of the available
variables and the structure of the data.

**Technical constraints**

The final product should remain readable and usable on standard desktop and laptop screens.
Because the project is implemented in a documented Quarto workflow, the design should favor
clarity, maintainability, and reproducibility over unnecessary technical complexity.

### Opportunities

The dataset offers several strong opportunities for visualization:

- comparison of incident **frequency** and **severity**
- analysis across multiple dimensions such as country, industry, year, attack type,
  vulnerability, and defense mechanism
- communication of both high-level patterns and more detailed comparisons
- development of a product that is useful for both exploratory analysis and management
  communication

### Risks and mitigation

| Risk | Relevance to project | Mitigation |
|:--|:--|:--|
| The dataset may be simplified or not fully representative of the wider global threat landscape. | Findings could be overinterpreted or presented too broadly. | Clearly communicate scope and limitations; frame results as patterns within the dataset rather than universal conclusions. |
| The visualization may become too complex because the dataset supports many dimensions. | Users may feel overwhelmed, especially management-oriented users. | Limit the number of views, prioritize the most relevant questions, and use progressive disclosure through filtering and sectioning. |
| The team may try to answer too many questions at once. | This could reduce focus and weaken the final product. | Define a clear analytical core and treat additional questions as optional extensions. |
| Some variables may be less informative than expected once explored in detail. | Certain intended views may not generate meaningful insight. | Use exploratory analysis early to validate promising dimensions and revise the visualization scope if necessary. |
| Technical implementation choices may take more time than expected. | Time could be lost on formatting or interaction details instead of analytical value. | Prefer robust, maintainable solutions in Quarto and keep interactivity selective rather than excessive. |

Overall, the situation assessment suggests that the project has a strong analytical basis
and a suitable technical setup, but will benefit most from disciplined scoping, careful
communication of data limitations, and a design that balances depth with clarity.

## Visualization Concept

The proposed visualization product is an interactive web-based report implemented within
the Quarto project website and published via GitHub Pages. Conceptually, it combines
elements of a **data story** and an **exploratory dashboard**: users should be able to
gain a quick overview of the most important patterns in the dataset while also being able
to compare selected dimensions in more detail.

The concept is designed to support both main user types identified in the user analysis.
Management-oriented users need a clear entry point, concise summaries, and easily
interpretable visual comparisons. More analytical users need the ability to compare
patterns across time, country, industry, attack type, vulnerability, defense mechanism,
and incident outcome measures such as financial loss, affected users, and resolution time.

### Concept overview

| Design aspect | Proposed concept | Intended value |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Product form | Quarto-based interactive web report with dashboard-like sections | Combines communicative clarity with analytical usefulness |
| Main structure | Overview, trends, threat profiles, defense/outcome comparison, key findings | Supports both fast orientation and deeper exploration |
| Core charts | KPI cards, line charts, sorted bar charts, grouped/stacked bars, heatmaps, boxplots or dot plots | Enables comparison, pattern detection, and interpretation across multiple dimensions |
| Interactivity | Light interactivity through filters, tooltips, and metric toggles | Adds analytical flexibility without overwhelming users |
| Narrative support | Section introductions, annotations, and short interpretive text | Improves accessibility and communication of findings |
| Medium | GitHub Pages-hosted Quarto website optimized for desktop and laptop use | Matches project setup and expected usage context |

### Product form

The product will take the form of a compact multi-section visual report rather than a
single-screen dashboard. This allows the project to maintain a clear narrative structure
while still offering interactive exploration where it adds value.

A likely structure is:

- an **overview section** with headline indicators and the most important first insights
- a **trend section** showing how incident patterns evolve over time
- a **comparison section** focusing on country and industry differences
- a **threat profile section** showing attack types, vulnerabilities, and attack sources
- an **outcome and defense section** comparing financial loss, affected users, and
  resolution time across selected categories
- a short **key findings section** summarizing the most relevant takeaways

This structure creates **cognitive and analytical value** by separating different
questions into manageable views, **communicative value** by guiding users through the
analysis, and **experiential value** by keeping the interface focused rather than crowded.

### Visual encodings

The visualization will primarily rely on chart types that support accurate comparison and
remain interpretable for users with medium data literacy:

- **KPI cards** for total incidents, average financial loss, average resolution time, or
  most frequent attack type
- **line charts** for developments over time
- **sorted bar charts** for comparing countries, industries, attack types, and
  vulnerabilities
- **grouped or stacked bar charts** for comparing category composition across countries
  or industries
- **heatmaps** for highlighting concentration patterns, for example attack type by
  industry or vulnerability by industry
- **boxplots or dot plots** for comparing distributions of financial loss or resolution
  time across defense mechanisms or attack categories

Country comparisons should preferably use **sorted bar charts** rather than a choropleth
map, because the dataset only includes a limited set of countries and precise comparison
is more important than geographical shape.

These encodings support **analytical value** by making rankings, distributions, trends,
and category relationships visible. They support **communicative value** by using familiar
visual forms, and **aesthetic value** by creating a consistent and readable visual system.

### Interactivity

Interactivity should be present, but selective and purposeful. The product should avoid
becoming a highly complex dashboard with too many controls.

Useful interactive features include:

- filters for **year**, **country**, **industry**, and **attack type**
- tooltips for exact values and contextual details
- simple metric switches where appropriate, for example between **incident count**,
  **financial loss**, and **resolution time**
- a clear **reset filters** option
- linked filtering across selected views where this can be implemented robustly

This level of interactivity relieves the users' pains identified earlier: analysts can
focus on relevant subsets of the data without manual aggregation, while managers can still
use the report without being overwhelmed by excessive controls.

### Narrative and annotation

The visualization should not rely on charts alone. Each major section should include a
brief textual introduction and, where useful, short annotations highlighting important
patterns. For example, annotations can point out:

- countries or industries with particularly high incident frequency
- attack categories associated with unusually high financial losses
- differences between frequent threats and severe threats
- defense mechanisms associated with shorter or longer recorded resolution times

The report should also include a short note on data limitations, making clear that the
visualization presents descriptive patterns within the dataset and does not establish
causal relationships or provide a complete picture of the global cybersecurity landscape.

This creates **communicative value** by reducing interpretation effort and helps build
**trust** through transparency.

### Target medium and integration

The product will be embedded in the GitHub Pages-hosted Quarto project website. It should
be optimized primarily for desktop and laptop screens, as these match the expected working
environment of both personas. The layout should remain readable on smaller laptop screens,
but the design priority is not mobile-first use.

This medium supports the project well because it allows transparent documentation,
reproducible implementation, and straightforward access for teaching staff and other
stakeholders.

### Mapping to user needs and project objectives

The concept directly addresses the previously identified user needs and project objectives:

- it supports **quick orientation** through summary views and KPI-style indicators
- it supports **deeper comparison** through multi-dimensional charts and filtering
- it distinguishes between **frequency** and **severity**, which is central to the data
  and to the intended analytical value of the project
- it helps analysts generate evidence-based insights while helping managers communicate
  the most relevant patterns more efficiently
- it remains realistic in scope by limiting interactivity to features that add clear value

Overall, the concept aims to create a visualization product that is analytically useful,
communicatively effective, and realistically implementable within the project timeframe.

## Project Plan

The project is structured in several consecutive but partly overlapping phases. The plan
follows the semester schedule and includes multiple feedback loops through coaching
sessions. This is important because the project is expected to evolve iteratively from the
initial project charta to the final visualization product and accompanying documentation.

The work begins with project understanding, stakeholder and user analysis, and the
preparation of the draft project charta. After the first coaching session, the focus shifts
towards data acquisition, data cleaning, exploratory analysis, and refinement of the
visualization concept. In the second half of the semester, the team concentrates on visual
encoding decisions, prototyping, implementation, evaluation, documentation, deployment, and
preparation for the final submission and presentation.

The timeline below is preliminary and may be adjusted slightly as the project develops,
especially after coaching sessions and during implementation.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-project-plan
%%| fig-cap: Preliminary project plan in the form of a Gantt chart.
gantt
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    tickInterval 1month
    axisFormat %d.%m.

    section Project Understanding
    Project kick-off and topic definition            :done, a1, 2026-02-24, 4d
    Stakeholder and user analysis                    :done, a2, 2026-03-02, 10d
    Draft project charta                             :active, a3, 2026-03-12, 9d
    Project charta submission                        :milestone, m1, 2026-03-20, 1d
    Concept feedback / coaching 1                    :milestone, m2, 2026-03-24, 1d

    section Data Acquisition and Exploration
    Acquire and validate data                        :a4, 2026-03-23, 4d
    Clean and prepare data                           :a5, 2026-03-27, 6d
    Exploratory data analysis                        :a6, 2026-04-02, 12d
    Consolidate data report findings                 :milestone, m3, 2026-04-14, 1d

    section Visual Encoding and Design
    Refine visualization concept                     :a7, 2026-03-25, 14d
    Chart selection and low-fidelity design          :a8, 2026-04-07, 10d
    Build overview and comparison views              :a9, 2026-04-16, 12d
    On-site coaching 2                               :milestone, m4, 2026-04-21, 1d
    Implement interactivity and linked filters       :a10, 2026-04-24, 12d
    Online coaching 3                                :milestone, m5, 2026-04-28, 1d
    Refine narrative, annotations, and styling       :a11, 2026-05-01, 10d
    On-site coaching 4                               :milestone, m6, 2026-05-05, 1d
    Complete visualization report                    :a12, 2026-05-08, 8d

    section Evaluation and Finalization
    Usability checks and revisions                   :a13, 2026-05-12, 10d
    Finalize documentation and deployment            :a14, 2026-05-19, 10d
    Prepare final presentation                       :a15, 2026-05-22, 10d
    Final project submission                         :milestone, m7, 2026-06-01, 1d

    section Closing Phase
    Presentation rehearsal and final adjustments     :a16, 2026-06-02, 10d
    Group presentation (tentative, late June)        :milestone, m8, 2026-06-23, 1d
```

## Roles and Contact Details

The project is developed by a three-person team. Responsibilities are assigned by main work
package, but the project is carried out collaboratively, with shared review and discussion
across all major phases.

**Project team**

| Name | Role | Main responsibilities | Contact |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Vithujan Shanmugaratnam | Project coordination, user perspective, documentation | Coordinates project progress; supports stakeholder and user analysis; contributes to documentation; helps ensure alignment between user needs, analytical scope, and final presentation | Email: shavit01@students.zhaw.ch<br>GitHub: s-vithu |
| Francis Lehner | Data analysis and visualization design | Supports data acquisition, data preparation, exploratory analysis, chart selection, and interpretation of analytical findings | Email: lehnefr1@students.zhaw.ch<br>GitHub: FrancisLehner |
| Brian Bernhauser | Implementation and technical integration | Supports implementation of the Quarto-based visualization product, interaction design, repository integration, and deployment preparation | Email: berbri01@students.zhaw.ch<br>GitHub: MuninsEye |

<div style="height: 1.5rem;"></div>
**Coaching and academic support**

| Name | Role | Contribution to project | Contact |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Manuel Dömer | Lecturer / course lead | Provides course requirements, academic framing, and overall evaluation context | doem@zhaw.ch |
| Wibke Weber | Coach / lecturer | Provides coaching feedback on concept, design decisions, and project development | webw@zhaw.ch |

### Shared responsibilities

All team members contribute to the discussion of findings, review of visual and textual
outputs, refinement after coaching sessions, and preparation of the final group
presentation. Final deliverables are therefore produced jointly, even where individual team
members take the lead on specific work packages.
